words: ROB MOORE: You were born on this place, weren't you?
NORLA CALLISON: I was born right here.

speaker: RM
words: When was it?

speaker: RM
words: You were born in the house?

speaker: NC
words: I was born in the old house that set right where this one sits. We

speaker: NC
words: moved the old house across the road and built this one in 1965. Star

speaker: NC
words: ted in in August and we got moved in- moved the stuff from across the

speaker: NC
words: road out of the old house into the new house. We got the final moving

speaker: NC
words: job finished Christmas Eve. Santy Claus, I tell you, was awful tired

speaker: NC
words: that night, he didn't do very much! Yeah, it is quite a chore to

speaker: NC
words: move stuff. Course, short move but nevertheless, we moved; carried

speaker: NC
words: most of it.

speaker: RM
words: Was there a country doctor that came out here?

speaker: NC
words: oh, yeah. When I was born Doctor Hunter was at Kendrick and he was

speaker: NC
words: the family doctor at that time. I don't know whether Doc Hunter died

speaker: NC
words: or whether he left. He had a ranch over on Potlatch Ridge, we can see

speaker: NC
words: it from the yard here, and then Doctor ^as the family doc

speaker: NC
words: tor for years and years. He was in Kendrick. And before he finally

speaker: NC
words: died there was a Doctor J. E. Hoyt, he come here, two of 'em had all

speaker: NC
words: they could do in Kendrick, and died. He was a Nova Scotian.

speaker: NC
words: Talked with quite a Nova Scotian brogue. And he was a great doctor

speaker: NC
words: with kids. He could throw his voice, and he could just charm kids.

speaker: NC
words: He . throw his voice, you know. And he'd get some kid that was not

speaker: NC
words: too sick, you know, and he'd get throwing his voice, you know, and

speaker: NC
words: he really could get next to the kids. (Chuckles) And then Dr. Hoyt,

speaker: NC
words: Dr. died and Dr. Hoyt stayed here a few years and he moved

speaker: NC
words: away and went to Spokane and set up practice in the Paulsen Building

speaker: NC
words: and he's dead now, been dead a number of years. Then we've had a

speaker: NC
words: number of doctors since that. Most of 'em didn't stay very long; just

speaker: NC
words: a few years. Dr. -was here twenty-five years, wasn't he?

speaker: NC
words: MRS. CALLISON: Longer than that.

speaker: NC
words: Well, Doug, you know, the oldest son was a baby and he carried him in

speaker: NC
words: his arms.

speaker: NC
words: MRS. C:   Well, he was here practically forty years.

speaker: NC
words: I guess it was about forty years, here in Kendrick, and he quit lastwell,

speaker: NC
words: he quit the last day of December finished out the year. The

speaker: NC
words: thirty-first day of December, he quit practicing. He still lives here.

speaker: NC
words: Him and his wife, Irene retired and lives just out of Kendrick.

speaker: RM
words: Well, this was your father's place, right?

speaker: NC
words: It originally was my grandfather's place; Sam, S.T.. He came out here

speaker: NC
words: from Kansas, settled here in 1888. I think it was the twelfth day of

speaker: NC
words: September that they arrived here. And Granddad and the family lived

speaker: NC
words: here until 1900, and Sam, that's my granddad moved to town, moved to

speaker: NC
words: Kendrick. And my dad then, and my mother got married and they

speaker: NC
words: lived here. And Sam lived in Kendrick til he died. He died in 1925.

speaker: RM
words: Well, why did Sam leave Kansas?

speaker: NC
words: Because the pastures were greener out here. And he lived over in the

speaker: NC
words: north east part of Kansas, little town called Lacine, on the

speaker: NC
words: River, and that was a tributary of the Missouri. And a neighbor of

speaker: NC
words: their's come out here with Al Roberts, that lives up here across the

speaker: NC
words: road from Melvin . They had been out here a year or two

speaker: NC
words: and they were neighbors back there and he wrote back and kept telling

speaker: NC
words: my granddad what a great country this was. So, the folks packed up

speaker: NC
words: and they moved out here and Al met 'em in Moscow. That was the end

speaker: NC
words: of the railroad at that time; and^met 'em out there with a wagon and

speaker: NC
words: they come down here and that's how they settled on American Ridge.

speaker: NC
words: Sam was quite a character, wasn't he?

speaker: NC
words: Well, he was an Irishman. He never come from Ireland but his ancestors

speaker: NC
words: come from Ireland. And had a fiery temper, nobody could deny that!

speaker: NC
words: He'd fight at the drop of the hat! Pretty good fighter and when

speaker: NC
words: was young pretty fair wrestler. Used to wrestle professionally. And

speaker: NC
words: he put up a pretty nasty scrap. If he couldn't lick 'em with his fists,

speaker: NC
words: why, he'd tie into 'em wrestling. (Chuckles) He was a pretty good

speaker: NC
words: man, he a lot of men bigger than he was. But he had a buzz

speaker: NC
words: saw temper.

speaker: NC
words: Did he get along with all his neighbors out here?

speaker: NC
words: Oh, yeah, he had no trouble with his neighbors. Somebody try to put

speaker: NC
words: something over on, that was different. Pretty rough making a living

speaker: NC
words: in Kansas in them days. He used to fish in the flUrViz-fnt River for

speaker: NC
words: catfish and in the fall and winter, why, they used to hunt prairie

speaker: NC
words: chickens. There were thousands of prairie chickens there and they

speaker: NC
words: used to hunt prairie chickens and^sell 'em on the market.A Kill prairie

speaker: NC
words: chickens and sell 'em. Ship 'em to Kansas City. And of course, they

speaker: NC
words: used them in the hotels and restaurants there. So they used to ship

speaker: NC
words: prairie chickens. Didn't get a fancy price out of em, but gave them

speaker: NC
words: a little money. They got two and a half a dozen. Two dollars and a

speaker: NC
words: half for a dozen prairie chickens. They done that for years. And

speaker: NC
words: the other neighbors did the same thing. They had to have something

speaker: NC
words: to live on. Most of 'em only had just a few acres around their farmstead

speaker: NC
words: that was in cultivation, all tall prairie grass.

speaker: NC
words: And they had lots of thumder and lightning storms there and also cy

speaker: NC
words: clones. And if that country ever got afire they didn't have any way

speaker: NC
words: to fight it. And that's why they had the farmstead in the middle of

speaker: NC
words: where they cultivated their land, so that the other would^ all burn out.

speaker: NC
words: Most of 'em didn't have over five or ten acres in cultivation. And

speaker: NC
words: they lived a pretty simple life; they done a lot of fishing and a

speaker: NC
words: lot of hunting. And they used to hunt antelope. Go out there about

speaker: NC
words: two day's travel from where Granddad lived, or Sam, and him and Al

speaker: NC
words: Roberts- I've heard Dad- and I can't think of the third guy- and

speaker: NC
words: J. B. Barriton,and he had a son living in Moscow. He used to be

speaker: NC
words: superintendent of the schools down here at Kendrick, years and years,

speaker: NC
words: along about 1908 and '09. I know I had a niece that graduated when

speaker: NC
words: he was superintendent.

speaker: NC
words: MRS. C:   Cousin, you mean, not niece.

speaker: NC
words: Yeah, cousin. And he come out here; him and Al and there was just a

speaker: NC
words: kind of a bunch of 'em from that little community, they all mi

speaker: NC
words: grated out here. They was all neighbors out here for years. Things

speaker: NC
words: was so much better out here that they moved West. It's human nature

speaker: NC
words: if you think you can better yourself, why, the pastures looked greener

speaker: NC
words: out here and they all come out here.

speaker: RM
words: Did they homestead when they got here?

speaker: NC
words: Most of 'em homesteaded. Quite a bit of this land was already taken

speaker: NC
words: up. My granddad bought a- this place was an Irishman, Jim Perkins,

speaker: NC
words: had homesteaded about a year before4heycome here, and he took TB

speaker: NC
words: and was dying of TB and granddad bought his preemption and finished

speaker: NC
words: proving up. The old shack- or the old sod shacks^across the draw

speaker: NC
words: over here; he had a fireplace made out of rock, just river rocks, and

speaker: NC
words: for years there we was always working one of them rocks out every once

speaker: NC
words: in a while, where the house set. That's how I come to know where the

speaker: NC
words: house used to be. And it was pretty here in the early days.

speaker: NC
words: Lots of prairie chickens here. Thousands of 'em here and they

speaker: NC
words: chicken all winter long. You had a corn patch. Everybody raised a

speaker: NC
words: few acres of corn and had some cows and two, three broodsows and they

speaker: NC
words: farmed with a footburner walking plow.A And if you didn't shuck your

speaker: NC
words: corn, if you left it out all winter by spring there wouldn't be no

speaker: NC
words: thing to shuck, because, because the birds they eat it all up. They

speaker: NC
words: walked along on the snow and the snow was just the right height so

speaker: NC
words: they could just peck the ears. So everybody ate chicken.

speaker: RM
words: Corn fed chickens, eh?

speaker: NC
words: That's right. And boy, they were good, oh, brother!

speaker: RM
words: So Sam Took up farming when he got here, too.

speaker: NC
words: He farmed some and done an awful lot of hunting and fishing. Coyote

speaker: NC
words: hunting and coon hunting and elk and deer hunting. Grandad was just

speaker: NC
words: a boy ten years old and him and his dad kept a plowing it up and put

speaker: NC
words: ting more in cultivation. And my father. Then it was all bunchgrass

speaker: NC
words: sod. And my dad then broke most of this place up with three Indian

speaker: NC
words: cayuses and a footburner.

speaker: RM
words: Didn't have to clear it, did he?

speaker: NC
words: Most of the timber was on the north slopes, in the draws. Like this

speaker: NC
words: draw back of the house. There's still trees back there but that come

speaker: NC
words: further up the hill. But most of this ground was all bunchgrass sod.

speaker: RM
words: Do you think that the ground was better ground then?

speaker: NC
words: Oh, yeah, it was better ground. It's been farmed all that time. They

speaker: NC
words: used to. Didn't raise any H crop at all. And they used to raise

speaker: NC
words: wheat and oats and flax. Lot of flax. And flax takes good ground.

speaker: NC
words: But they got the ground so foul that they had to quit raising flax.

speaker: NC
words: Flax would make, oh, twelve, fifteen bushels an acre. And it was

speaker: NC
words: pretty good money in those days raising flax.

speaker: RM
words: What was the flax used for?

speaker: NC
words: Oh, mostly linen, wasn't it, Mom?

speaker: NC
words: Mrs. C. Well, did they thresh—?

speaker: NC
words: They threshed it; sold it; sacked it.

speaker: NC
words: MRS. C:   Well, what you had must have been for seed, because they didn't use

speaker: NC
words: any of the fiber- you never sold that.

speaker: NC
words: Well, Granddad did.

speaker: NC
words: MRSC: Did he sell the fiber?

speaker: NC
words: Sold the finished- the threshed seed.

speaker: NC
words: MRS. C:   Well, that was for seed to plant somewhere else.

speaker: NC
words: They could take it and process it.

speaker: NC
words: MRS. C:   Well, they made linseed oil out of it, for one thing. But you^take,

speaker: NC
words: the stocks is what they use to make linen. Had to take that and-

speaker: NC
words: Well, that flax was the slickest stuff I ever saw. You ever see any

speaker: NC
words: flax? Hold it in your hand? It's so slick! It shines just like

speaker: NC
words: it'd been polished or oiled or kind of . The slickest stuff you

speaker: NC
words: ever saw. Yes, they raised flax and hay, their crops was all spring

speaker: NC
words: crops, til the ground got to foul with wild oats they - to quit

speaker: NC
words: putting a spring crop in, then they started raising fall grain. But

speaker: NC
words: use, fertilizer in them days, and shucks you could just plow it up—

speaker: NC
words: when they first come they seeded by hand. My dad seeded for years

speaker: NC
words: there- they'd get a little hack, or like a buggy and take the top off

speaker: NC
words: and one guy'd drive the wagon and the guy that was seeding, he'd get

speaker: NC
words: in the back end of the wagon and set down and have a washtub- they

speaker: NC
words: had grain in the washtub, and this guy that would drive, he could

speaker: NC
words: see the marksfwhSe he went around before and they'd seed with both

speaker: NC
words: hands; this way. Just reach down and get a handful and just seed and

speaker: NC
words: heck a guy could seed twenty-five or thirty feet a strip and do a

speaker: NC
words: good job of seeding if the guy driving didn't drive^far and he missed.

speaker: NC
words: It depended a let on the driver, if he tried to take too big a swath

speaker: NC
words: you was going to leave a strip that wouldn't be seeded. They seeded

speaker: NC
words: by hand for years. And then they finally got a drill. That's quite

speaker: NC
words: an improvement when they got a grain drill.

speaker: RM
words: You said the wild oats came up.

speaker: NC
words: Well, the wild oats got started. Them wild oats, they got some seed

speaker: NC
words: from California, was how the wild oats got started in here. Got the

speaker: NC
words: seed from California and got wild oats started. And of course, they

speaker: NC
words: just multiplied, just like nobody's business. And when they got fouled

speaker: NC
words: like that- course they didn't have alfalfa them days. They cut all

speaker: NC
words: this wild oats, patches where the wild oats was thickest, cut that

speaker: NC
words: for hay; cut it green for hay. Made good hay. But it just went from

speaker: NC
words: that, you know. Hand an old threshing machine here, an odl handfeed.

speaker: NC
words: I can remember seeing 'em using that thing, you know. They'd haul it

speaker: NC
words: in and put the bundles on it. The first heading they'd lose, you

speaker: NC
words: know, lose grain, and then they got to binding it and they had a

speaker: NC
words: handfeed machine, had a handfeed grain machine and they had an old

speaker: NC
words: handfeed bean thresher. And Mr. Nick Brocke, the father of the Brockes

speaker: NC
words: that have the seed house down here, he was the man that owned the bean

speaker: NC
words: thresher. And I've seen him time and again stand up there, he had

speaker: NC
words: black beard, he'd have a corncob pipe in his mouth and it was

speaker: NC
words: just as apt to be upside down as it was like it should be, and most

speaker: NC
words: of the time wasn't lit but he had the taste. And he'd stand there

speaker: NC
words: that dirt aboiling out of that thing right in his face, and he had

speaker: NC
words: a peg in his left hand and he'd reach over on the table there, the

speaker: NC
words: table they set the beans on, he'd reach over this way and bring one

speaker: NC
words: in and push 'em down in the cylinder, see. And do that all day and

speaker: NC
words: that dust just aboiling right out in his face. He'd get just as

speaker: NC
words: dirty as a man could get. And he was a nice old gentleman. Fine fel

speaker: NC
words: low. He lived down the road here about, oh, he bull* about two miles.

speaker: RM
words: Did he hire that bean threshing rig around?

speaker: NC
words: Oh, he owned it and then he charged you so much a sack for threshing

speaker: NC
words: your beans. Most people in them days didn't have- well, if aman

speaker: NC
words: had twenty acres of beans he had quite a crop. And a big day's run

speaker: NC
words: was, oh, seventy-five or eighty sacks a day. That was a good run.

speaker: NC
words: And he done a good job of threshing, too. Clean. Little old threshing

speaker: NC
words: machine wasn't any longer than from here to the wall.

speaker: RM
words: Was it run by horse power?

speaker: NC
words: No, it had a big gas engine that they pulled with a team of horses.

speaker: NC
words: It wasn't selfpropelled; hooked a team on and pulled that engine.

speaker: NC
words: Run it with a big gas engine.

speaker: NC
words: You'd bring the beans to it, wouldn't you?

speaker: NC
words: No, just set the machine you see, set this up and this big gas enthat's

speaker: NC
words: engine furnish the power on a belt.

speaker: RM
words: So the horses would pull from job to job.

speaker: NC
words: Whenever you moved, you had to have horses on the separator as well

speaker: NC
words: as on the engine. And then when they got to threshing grain they

speaker: NC
words: got the traction engine, where they got through they'd just

speaker: NC
words: throw the belt off and run out and turn around and hook onto the

speaker: NC
words: separator and take off. They didn't do it like that at first, they

speaker: NC
words: had to have a team on the separator and on the engine, both.

speaker: NC
words: MRS. C:   We brought the beans in from the field

speaker: NC
words: Oh, then sometimes they stacked 'em. Sometimes you hauled them with

speaker: NC
words: a wagon, and the wagonrack on the wagon and haul beans and lots of

speaker: NC
words: times they'd put 'em in a stack. And they couldn't thresh 'em for

speaker: NC
words: a while so they'd go through the sweat. They'd go through a sweat

speaker: NC
words: and then dry out. But most of the beans was threshed out .the

speaker: NC
words: field , because quite a few stacked, because you could thresh them

speaker: NC
words: anytime and cover 'em over with straw and you could thresh them in

speaker: NC
words: the middle of the winter. I know one time I threshed beans for

speaker: NC
words: Clifford Davidson down here- George Davidson owned a separator and

speaker: NC
words: he was a brother of Cliff's, and there was Anderson and Andy

speaker: NC
words: Cox, you remember Andy down on the Ridge here where the white barns

speaker: NC
words: is, and another fella and two and myself pitched beans on New Year's

speaker: NC
words: Day. And cold, man, it was cold!fright, sunny day- we had to work

speaker: NC
words: hard or freeze to death. (Chuckles) Threshed 'em on New Year's Day.

speaker: RM
words: Did you follow the threshing crews around the country?

speaker: RM
words: a company machine

speaker: NC
words: No. My dad- there was six men bought- and my dad was one

speaker: NC
words: of the six. There was George Davidson, he was the separator man and

speaker: NC
words: he was the man that kept the machine going. We bought a separator

speaker: NC
words: had an old -gas tractor, and it was the kind that you hook on

speaker: NC
words: the machine and it was the traction. You'd hook on the separator,

speaker: NC
words: and we an Alden-Taylor threshing machine. And there was George

speaker: NC
words: Davidson and Frank Roberts and Walt Bigham and Wade Keene and Byron

speaker: NC
words: Davidson and my Dad that bought that first company threshing machine.

speaker: NC
words: And they ran that thing for years and years and once in a while one

speaker: NC
words: would drop out and somebody'd buy his share. But they used th atthey

speaker: NC
words: bought it in 1915 and they run that company thresher until

speaker: NC
words: 1933. That was the last year we used it then we went into combines.

speaker: NC
words: 1935 was the first year of combines. Company threshing machine busted

speaker: NC
words: up and pretty near all of 'em bought a combine or two guys a go

speaker: NC
words: together and buy a combine between 'em. Then pretty soon each one

speaker: NC
words: of 'em had . The places kept getting bigger.

speaker: NC
words: When you were threshing with the company threshing machine, could

speaker: NC
words: you stay with sacked grain?

speaker: NC
words: No, when we bought that company threshing machine we went to bulk

speaker: NC
words: grain. Each man that had a share in the machine, why, he had a

speaker: NC
words: wagon and a tank- a wagon with a boattank bed on it, and they

speaker: NC
words: 'M haul from a hundred to about a hundred and forty bushels.

speaker: NC
words: Usually five horses. Each rig would haul to Kendrick oat mill. They

speaker: NC
words: had a concrete elevator there and that's the only place that would

speaker: NC
words: take the grain. That's how the sacks went out here and^bulk came

speaker: NC
words: in. Of course, everything's bulk now and has been for years and

speaker: NC
words: years.

speaker: RM
words: Did you ever drive wagon?

speaker: NC
words: I never drove a grain wagon in my life. I was on the pitching end.

speaker: NC
words: I was with the machine, my dad drove the grain wagon. I started to

speaker: NC
words: hauling when I was twelve years old. And I hauled until 1926.

speaker: NC
words: Hauled the bundle wagon., And when we got through cutting our grain

speaker: NC
words: if a neighbor wanted us to cut for him, we'd cut for higi. But always

speaker: NC
words: cut for everybody in the company first. And they'd start in at one

speaker: NC
words: end one year and at the other end the other year. We were kind of

speaker: NC
words: strung out on the Ridge here. And that way nobody had to always be

speaker: NC
words: last every year. We'd rotate. And in 1926 Dad bought- they bought

speaker: NC
words: a one torn Chevrolet truck.. And I got elevated from a bundler hauler

speaker: NC
words: to a truck driver. I drove that old Chewy. And there was part of

speaker: NC
words: 'em that was hauling with a wagon for a year or two. Then they all

speaker: NC
words: went to trucks. And of course, the trucks got bigger. The old one

speaker: NC
words: trucks didn't last very long. And that's about the way it was.

speaker: NC
words: Of course then we quit raising beans. Beans, we quit them years ago.

speaker: NC
words: Beans was a good ground builder, but the worst trouble raising beans

speaker: NC
words: was you had to get your ground in shape to hold your moisture and

speaker: NC
words: that encouraged the erosion. Wouldn't have been so bad if you could

speaker: NC
words: have put the straw back on the ground and worked it in like you do

speaker: NC
words: peas. But they hauled the beans in and threshed 'em in the stack

speaker: NC
words: and they made excellent cattle feed, and they fed 'em to cattle.

speaker: NC
words: Cattle is crazy about bean . Good feed. So they just quit the

speaker: NC
words: beansbecause we couldn't compete with South Idaho irrigated beans.

speaker: NC
words: We'd get well, five, six and seven sacks here was a good yield, dry

speaker: NC
words: land farming, you know. And in South Idaho, why, they can get twenty

speaker: NC
words: sacks to the acre. We couldn't compete with the price, we didn't

speaker: NC
words: have no volume. We had to go out of business, that's all. Between

speaker: NC
words: that and being bad for the ground to erode, if it kept it up

speaker: NC
words: long we wouldn't have no farm. Just wash away.

speaker: RM
words: You said Sam Callison did a lot of hunting and fishing around here?

speaker: NC
words: Oh, yeah, he went everywhere. Sometimes I think he done too much.

speaker: NC
words: He should have stayed home and tended to business a little more, but

speaker: NC
words: he had a lot of fun hunting and fishing.

speaker: RM
words: Was the hunting and fishing pretty good around here?

speaker: NC
words: Oh, boy, it was second to none. Second to none. They could kill

speaker: NC
words: deer right here on the ranch when they first come here. And then

speaker: NC
words: there was a while when there's wasn't many deer around here and they

speaker: NC
words: went way back in the mountains to hunt deer and elk. The fishing

speaker: NC
words: was fantastic. Right down here in Bear Creek, I used to ride down

speaker: NC
words: there of an evening with a cayuse, why, you'd go down there and catch

speaker: NC
words: oh, fifty, sixty trout there in an hour, hour and a half. They

speaker: NC
words: weren't big, just nice pansized fish, you know. Dandy fish.

speaker: NC
words: No limit?

speaker: NC
words: No limit. Everybody caught what they needed, they didn't waste

speaker: NC
words: 'em. There was lots of fish.

speaker: RM
words: Did they smoke the fish or dry the fish?

speaker: NC
words: No. Well, sometimes we'd can 'em. We'd can 'em. But if you want

speaker: NC
words: real good fishing, well, you go up above Kendrick about three miles

speaker: NC
words: above the mouth of Cedar Creek there, there's a little creek called

speaker: NC
words: Rock Creek. Go up on Texas Ridge usually about four fellows; I got

speaker: NC
words: in on that the last two, three trips up there- I was a kid- they'd

speaker: NC
words: take a couple of teams and hacks and go up there about four men, two

speaker: NC
words: to each rig and they'd go up on the old Van Lantle place and set

speaker: NC
words: their rigs about a mile and a half past where Roy Glen lives now,

speaker: NC
words: and unhitch horses, and pack our beds and what little eats we

speaker: NC
words: took along and go down the canyon there, the Potlatch Canyon and

speaker: NC
words: we'd camp out one night. We'd fish in the evening and the next mor

speaker: NC
words: ning you'd come home. Well, we used to buy coal oil in five gallon

speaker: NC
words: cans and we'd usually come back with aboutAfive gallon cans full of

speaker: NC
words: trout. And Salt 'em. We'd have fish all summer. They were bigger

speaker: NC
words: trout than what we got down here. Well, they was just about the

speaker: NC
words: length of a table knife, as I remember when I went there. Once

speaker: NC
words: while you'd get a heck of a big one. I know Dad one time got one up

speaker: RM
words: Tell me the story about Martin Thomas and the feist.

speaker: RM
words: Oh, yes, he was my uncle. He homesteaded over in the Genesee Country

speaker: RM
words: before he come over here, was in the early '70's. He was a bache

speaker: RM
words: lor, he hadn't got married. Just him and his feist and they had a

speaker: RM
words: little old sod shack; one room shack over there. And the house set

speaker: RM
words: up on the ground on blocks for a ways and then he had a board tacked

speaker: RM
words: around the bottom. And there were quite a few wolves in the Genesee

speaker: RM
words: Country in days and these wolves would come up next to his house

speaker: RM
words: because they could see the light andjstand out there and bark at it.

speaker: RM
words: And this feist he was awful brave, he was inside the house, you know,

speaker: RM
words: with my uncle and he'd just bark and he wanted to get outside awful

speaker: RM
words: bad to get at these wolves. And one night they got so close that

speaker: RM
words: my uncle decided he'd get the gun and open the door and he thought

speaker: RM
words: with the light in the house he could see 'em out and maybe get a shot

speaker: RM
words: at 'em. He opened the door and the feist shot between his legs and

speaker: RM
words: went on out and he didn't come back. Pretty soon he shut the door

speaker: RM
words: and he heard the feist coming. He was just squealing bloody murder

speaker: RM
words: and of course, these wolves was just about to get him and he hit

speaker: RM
words: that side of the house just kerbang and grunting and squealing and

speaker: RM
words: he went under the house. Well, he didn't see the feist anymore and

speaker: RM
words: the next morning he got up and there was a hole and the hole wasn't

speaker: RM
words: quite as big as the feist and he called and the feist- he couldn't

speaker: RM
words: get out. He got through the hole with these wolves after him but

speaker: RM
words: he couldn't get out, and he said this hole, the dog was kind of a

speaker: RM
words: red-brown dog, he said this hole was just lined with red dog hair

speaker: RM
words: (Chuckles) So he had to tear a board off to get the dog out from

speaker: RM
words: under the house.
